coredata.dc:title: Enhancing the visible light absorption of MOF-808 by surface decoration with sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots: Highly efficient photocatalyst for improved photocatalytic water remediation
coredata.dc:creator: Rahmani, Milad
Abbasi, Alireza
Hosseini, Mahdiyeh-Sadat
coredata.dc:description: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have aroused growing attention in the domain of photocatalysis. However, the photocatalytic performance of MOFs has been severely restricted by their fast recombination of photo-generated electrons and low utilization of solar energy. Combining MOFs with narrow-gap semiconductors can help to overcome their functional shortcomings. In this work, MOF-808 and sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (S,N-CQDs) were synthesized using a simple solvothermal technique. Then, novel MOF-808/S,N-CQDs(x) (MS-x) composites were fabricated through a simple solvent-deposition technique to decorate various amounts of S,N-CQDs on the surface of MOF-808. The morphology of MOF-808 and MS-0.5 composite, along with the lattice spacing, and size distribution of decorated S,N-CQDs on MOF-808 surface were investigated by SEM and HR-TEM analyses. According to the UV–Vis DRS and Tauc plots findings, MOF-808 was not photo-active in visible light region because of its large band gap energy (3.85 eV). In contrast, the photocatalytic performance of the prepared composites in Rhodamine B (RhB) degradation was significantly enhanced compared with MOF-808 under visible light irradiation. The results confirmed that S,N-CQDs play a key role in enhancing the visible light absorption efficiency. MS-0.5 composite exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity among the other composites and demonstrated high RhB photodegradation up to 89 % under visible light and 95 % under sunlight irradiation in optimal conditions. According to the kinetic studies, MS-0.5 composite had the highest rate constant, 29.67 and 6.93 times greater than S,N-CQDs and MOF-808, respectively. The scavenger tests revealed that h+ and OH radicals were the main active intermediates in the photocatalytic reaction. In addition, MS-0.5 composite exhibited excellent reusability because only a minor loss of photoactivity was observed after four reaction cycles.

head.ce:title: Enhancing the visible light absorption of MOF-808 by surface decoration with sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots: Highly efficient photocatalyst for improved photocatalytic water remediation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, P.O. Box: 14155-6455, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, P.O. Box: 14155-6455, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have aroused growing attention in the domain of photocatalysis. However, the photocatalytic performance of MOFs has been severely restricted by their fast recombination of photo-generated electrons and low utilization of solar energy. Combining MOFs with narrow-gap semiconductors can help to overcome their functional shortcomings. In this work, MOF-808 and sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (S,N-CQDs) were synthesized using a simple solvothermal technique. Then, novel MOF-808/S,N-CQDs(x) (MS-x) composites were fabricated through a simple solvent-deposition technique to decorate various amounts of S,N-CQDs on the surface of MOF-808. The morphology of MOF-808 and MS-0.5 composite, along with the lattice spacing, and size distribution of decorated S,N-CQDs on MOF-808 surface were investigated by SEM and HR-TEM analyses. According to the UV–Vis DRS and Tauc plots findings, MOF-808 was not photo-active in visible light region because of its large band gap energy (3.85 eV). In contrast, the photocatalytic performance of the prepared composites in Rhodamine B (RhB) degradation was significantly enhanced compared with MOF-808 under visible light irradiation. The results confirmed that S,N-CQDs play a key role in enhancing the visible light absorption efficiency. MS-0.5 composite exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity among the other composites and demonstrated high RhB photodegradation up to 89 % under visible light and 95 % under sunlight irradiation in optimal conditions. According to the kinetic studies, MS-0.5 composite had the highest rate constant, 29.67 and 6.93 times greater than S,N-CQDs and MOF-808, respectively. The scavenger tests revealed that h+ and OH radicals were the main active intermediates in the photocatalytic reaction. In addition, MS-0.5 composite exhibited excellent reusability because only a minor loss of photoactivity was observed after four reaction cycles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, people have become more concerned about water pollution. The sewage produced by industries such as the textile industry contains various poisonous and hazardous chemicals to the environment and living beings, making it a significant contributor to water pollution [1]. Rhodamine B (RhB) is one of the most hazardous dyes in textile wastewater due to its great stability and non-biodegradability, despite its widespread usage as a textile colorant [2]. RhB is a carcinogenic dye that has been known to have a variety of adverse health effects in people and animals, including skin irritation, nausea, and eyes infection [3]. In order to achieve the requirements of pollution control, developing an appropriate treatment method is crucial. Therefore, a variety of techniques, such as biological and physicochemical have been employed for removing dyes from wastewater. Because physicochemical procedures utilize chemicals, they cause secondary pollution. On the other hand, biological techniques take a long time to operate, and experimental circumstances affect these methods [4,5]. Photocatalysis reactions are one kind of environmental remediation technology that can use clean and renewable sunlight to start redox processes without causing secondary pollution [6]. For example, Yang et al. [7] prepared a novel TiO2/g-C3N4 S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst by homogeneously embedding hollow octahedral TiO2 nanoparticles (HOT) in ultra-thin porous g-C3N4 nanosheets (UPCN). The HOT/UPCN0.4 exhibited the highest chemical oxygen demand (COD) removal rate for real liquor brewing wastewater (LBW), reaching 50.4 %, which was 2.2 times greater than that of Degussa P25 (23.4 %).

Recently, there has been an increase in the development of metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), which are porous-structured materials consisting of metal clusters and organic linkers [8]. Thanks to the fascinating properties of MOFs, including wide surface area and tolerable structures, enabled researchers to apply them in catalysis and photocatalysis fields [9]. Some MOFs have been shown to behave like semiconductors when exposed to light and metal clusters taking on the conduction band (CB) role and the organic ligand acting as the valence band (VB) [10]. Nevertheless, some MOFs are excited by UV light due to their large band gaps, limiting their effectiveness as photocatalysts when exposed to visible light. Also, some issues including weak conductivity and fast recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes cause low photocatalytic efficiency [11]. Combining MOFs with narrow-gap semiconductors, like quantum dots (QDs)-MOF composites, has received a lot of attention and has become a new focus of study because the prepared composites have the potential to overcome the limitations mentioned earlier [12].

The MOF-808 is a three-dimensional porous MOF with excellent stability, large porosity, and high surface area [13]. Nevertheless, MOF-808 can only be used under ultraviolet light irradiation because of its large band gap. As noted earlier, the fabrication of composites using MOFs and semiconductors can expand the light absorption area. For instance, Wang et al. [14] showed that MOF-808/AgI composite had a high photodegradation efficiency of 93.65 % for tetracycline (TC) antibiotic, while the pristine MOF was able to degrade only 47.2 % of TC. In another example, Samy et al. [15] used MOF-808/CNTs for the degradation of diazinon and carbamazepine (CBZ). The removal of CBZ was increased from 68 % to 91 % by utilizing 0.3 % CNT/MOF-808 instead of pristine MOF-808. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a novel class of carbon-based nanomaterials that have attracted significant attention due to their desirable qualities. These properties are strong electron conductivity, low cost, small particle size and chemical stability [16]. CQDs could be supported by MOFs for enhanced photocatalytic performance due to their high specific surface area. CQDs with good electro-conductivity could help in the efficient separation of charge carriers, if they are supported on MOFs [16,17]. Generally, CQDs can function as a photosensitizer, spectrum converter, or electron receptor in many different photocatalytic systems when used as a co-catalyst [18]. Doping CQDs with nonmetallic elements, such as N, P, S, etc., enables them to enhance their optical stability, catalytic activity and reduces the band gap [19].

To the best of our knowledge, composites based on MOF-808 decorated with CQDs have never been fabricated. In this study, the photocatalytic activity of novel MOF-808/S,N-CQDs(x) (MS-x) composites for degradation of RhB, as a model of aquatic pollutant, was investigated for the first time. The hydrothermal technique was used to synthesize photostable and affordable S,N-CQDs, then composites of MOF-808 with various amounts of S,N-CQDs were easily synthesized at room temperature via a solvent-deposition technique. The photocatalytic performance of MOF-808 and MS-x composites were investigated under visible light illumination, and the findings revealed that the composites had much greater capability for removing RhB than MOF-808. Also, the effects of photocatalyst dosage, H2O2 amount, pH of solution, and RhB concentration were evaluated to identify the optimum conditions. Furthermore, the photocatalytic mechanism and reusability of MS-0.5, which showed the best photo-degradation capability among other composites, were assessed. Findings showed excellent stability and reusability for MS-0.5 during four cycles.
The supplementary information includes the lists of all materials and instruments that were used.

MOF-808 was obtained via the solvothermal technique in accordance with the previous report [20]. First, 0.25 mmol of trimesic acid (BTC) and 0.75 mmol of ZrCl4 were added to a mixture of formic acid and DMF (8:8 mL). Following that, the mixture was sonicated until a clear solution was obtained. Next, the solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and heated to 120 °C for 24 h, followed by cooling down to room temperature. The white colored solid was collected by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 15 min, then magnetically stirred with 30 mL of DMF and acetone separately for 3 h at ambient conditions. After that, soxhlet extraction with methanol was done overnight.

In a typical one-step hydrothermal synthesis, 1 mmol of citric acid (CA) and 3 mmol of thiourea (TU) were added to 5 mL distilled water(DW), and the mixture was stirred until it became clear [21]. Afterwards, the mixture was transferred into a 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 4 h. The obtained solution in the previous step was purified by four rounds of filtering using a 0.22 μm membrane, and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 1 h. Then, the solution was further purified by dialyzing against deionized water (DW) and finally, a pale green solution of S,N-CQDs was achieved.

0.1 g of MOF-808 was added to 10 mL of DW at room temperature. The mixture was then stirred until complete dispersion was achieved. Next, a specified volume (0.2, 0.5, 1, 1.5 mL) of S,N-CQDs was poured into the mixture and then held under magnetic stirring for 20 h. The final product (recorded as MS-x, where x denotes the volume of S,N-CQDs added) was dried at 100 °C for 5 h and washed six times with DW. Scheme 1 depicts the steps of MS-x preparation.

The photocatalytic performance of MS-x composites was evaluated under visible light source (four white LED bulbs with a power of 15 W). In a typical procedure, 50 mL of RhB solution (20 ppm) was mixed with 20 mg of MS-x composites. The mixture was kept stirring for 30 min to reach adsorption–desorption equilibrium prior to illumination. Subsequently, a small quantity of hydrogen peroxide (30 % w/w) was added into the mixture and exposed to visible light irradiation. Every 30 min throughout the photoreaction, 3 mL of the solution was taken out and centrifuged to separate the photocatalyst. RhB concentrations were obtained utilizing UV–vis spectrophotometer at absorbance wavelength of 553 nm.

FT-IR spectroscopy confirmed that MOF-808 and MS-0.5 were synthesized successfully. In MOF-808 spectrum (Fig. 1 a), the vibrational peaks at 649, 1108, 1342, 1420, 1571 cm−1, a broad peak at 3460 cm−1, and peaks ranging from 2900 to 2980 cm−1 could be attributed to Zr-O vibration, C-O and O–H stretching (in the aromatic structure), symmetric and asymmetric stretching of COO–, hydroxyl groups stretching vibration of adsorbed water, and C–H stretching, respectively [22]. Three new peaks at 1382, 1432, and 1625 cm−1 were observed in MS-0.5 spectrum owing to C = S stretching, C-N bending, and stretching vibration of C = O in CONH, respectively [21,23]. Moreover, the detected peak at 649 cm−1 seems more stronger than MOF-808 because of overlapping Zr-O vibration in MOF with C-S stretching in S,N-CQDs, demonstrating the successful loading of S,N-CQDs on the surface of MOF-808 [24].

The crystalline properties of the MOF-808 and MS-0.5 composite were evaluated by XRD analysis, and the related patterns were illustrated in Fig. 1b. The MOF-808 was successfully synthesized, as shown by the compatibility of the MOF-808 diffraction peaks with the simulated pattern derived from CIF data [25]. XRD patterns of MS-0.5 and MOF-808 were similar, indicating that the crystalline phase of MOF-808 was maintained after loading S,N-CQDs on MOF surface. Furthermore, the peaks intensity of MS-0.5 composite were reduced because of the amorphous nature of S,N-CQDs and their coverage on the surface of MOF-808 [26]. It is worth noting that the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of MS-0.5 did not exhibit any characteristic peaks for S,N-CQDs. Furthermore, the positions of the observed peaks in MS-0.5 composite were barely changed compared to MOF-808. The observed phenomenon can be ascribed to the small amount of S,N-CQDs present in the composite. This finding strongly suggested that the low amount of S,N-CQDs did not affect the crystal structure of MOF-808 [23].

N2 adsorption–desorption analysis was carried out for both MOF-808 and MS-0.5 composite, as seen in Fig. 2 . The nitrogen gas absorption isotherm of MOF-808 showed a Langmuir-type shape (type I), characterized by a steep slope at low relative pressures (P/P0 = 0.1) which remained almost constant with increasing relative pressure, indicating the characteristics of microporous compounds [27]. Similarly, MS-0.5 composite exhibited a type I isotherm, with a sharp slope at low pressure (P/P0 = 0.05). Since S,N-CQDs were located on the surface of MOF-808, N2 gas uptake was reduced. Furthermore, because of the same reason, the BET surface area of MOF-808 (1751 m2/g) is 2.66 times greater than MS-0.5 (658.5 m2/g). Also, the total pore volume of MS-0.5 composite (0.3931 cm3.g−1) is relatively lower than that of MOF-808 (0.8027 cm3.g−1), indicating that part of S,N-CQDs may occupy MOF's pores [28].

Further evidence of the successful synthesis of MS-0.5 composite was investigated by means of EDX analysis and elemental mapping, shown in Fig. 3 . EDX spectroscopy revealed the presence of C, N, O, S, and Zr in the MS-0.5 composite structure. Also, the uniform distribution of elements was confirmed via elemental mapping analysis, which demonstrated that S,N-CQDs were appropriately distributed on the surface of MOF-808.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to determine the composition and confirm the presence of desired elements in the MS-0.5 composite. The XPS full survey spectrum of photocatalyst, as shown in Fig. 4 a, presented five peaks at 530, 400, 285, 180 and 163 eV, which corresponded to O 1 s, N 1 s, C 1 s, Zr 3d and S 2p, respectively. The O 1 s spectrum can be deconvoluted into four different peaks (Fig. 4b), which were ascribed to the signals of Zr-O (529.6 eV), C-O-C (530.5 eV), C-OH (531.7 eV) and O-C = O (532.9 eV) [29]. Four characteristic peaks can be seen for C 1 s high resolution spectrum (Fig. 4c) at binding energies of 284.2, 285.4, 287.8 and 288.5 eV corresponding to C–C/C = C, C(sp3) in C-N, C-S, and C-O, C = O and O-C = O, respectively [30]. High resolution spectrum of Zr 3d (Fig. 4d) revealed two different peaks at binding energies of 181.7 and 183.5 eV assigned to 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively [29]. The S 2p spectrum can also be deconvoluted into two peaks (Fig. 4e), which were attributed to the signals of S 2p3/2 (163.1 eV) and S 2p1/2 (164.4 eV) [31]. Finally, the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4f) showed two peaks at 399.8 and 401.8 eV, attributed to the pyrrolic N (C-N-C) and graphitic N or N–H bands of S,N-CQDs, respectively [30].

Based on the FT-IR and XPS results, no new chemical bonds were observed after preparing the composite. Accordingly, zeta potential analysis was conducted to assess the surface charge of the MOF-808 and MS-0.5 composite and understand the type of interaction between MOF-808 and S,N-CQDs (Fig. S2). The MS-0.5 composite showed a zeta potential of −30.9 mV, which was attributed to the surface decoration of MOF-808 with S,N-CQDs. S,N-CQDs have various functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, etc., on their surface [32]. These functional groups, especially oxygenic groups, made the surface charge of the composite negative [33]. On the other hand, in zirconium-based MOFs, there is a tendency in the formation of Zr-OH2 + structure in an incomplete-coordinated Zr-O cluster [34]. Pure MOF-808 exhibited a high positive zeta potential of + 60.7 mV. Consequently, the positive surface charge of MOF-808 and the negative surface charge of S,N-CQDs were responsible for building electrostatic interactions between the cationic Zr-O cluster of MOF-808 and the oxygenic groups of S,N-CQDs. Additionally, it was possible that π-π stacking interactions occurred between the aromatic regions of S,N-CQDs and the aromatic rings in the trimesate ligands [32]. Therefore, it could be inferred that both π-π and electrostatic interactions were the main forces for the adsorption of S,N-CQDs by MOF-808.

The morphology of MOF-808 and MS-0.5 composite was determined through FESEM analysis. MOF-808 (Fig. 5 a and b) exhibited a regular octahedron with a uniform size of about 200–300 nm, which is in accordance with prior observation [14]. Also, the FESEM images of MS-0.5 composite (Fig. 5c and d) showed the same morphology of parent MOF, but surface of the composite became smoother in comparison with MOF-808. This can be attributed to the existence of S,N-CQDs on the surface of MOF-808.

Fig. 5e depicted the HR-TEM image of the as-synthesized MS-0.5 composite. The image revealed that sphere-shaped S,N-CQDs had a favorable dispersion with a narrow size distribution ranging from 4 to 6 nm, which the size was determined from HR-TEM image. As shown in Fig. 5f, the lattice spacing was estimated to be nearly 0.31 nm, related to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, which provided strong evidence for the existence of S,N-CQDs on the surface of MOF-808 [35].

The optical absorption abilities of MOF-808 and MS-0.5 composite were investigated by analyzing their UV–Vis DRS spectra (Fig. 6 a). MOF-808 was just able to absorb UV light region with an absorption edge of roughly 340 nm because of its wide band gap. The MS-0.5 composite showed noticeable absorption in the visible light area compared to MOF-808, showing that S,N-CQDs successfully extended the MOF-808 optical response range to the visible light region. Fig. S1 shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of S,N-CQDs with three absorption peaks at approximately 260, 336, and 600 nm. These peaks corresponded to the π → π* transition of C = C, and the n → π* transitions of C = O and S = O, respectively. The presence of an absorption peak in the visible area made S,N-CQDs viable candidates for use as solar absorber agents [21]. Additionally, the band gap energies of the MOF-808 and MS-0.5 composite were calculated by using the Tauc formula, (αhʋ)2 = B(hʋ - Eg), where band gap energy, absorption coefficient and photon energy are denoted as Eg, α, and hʋ, respectively [36]. According to the obtained Tauc plots shown in Fig. 6b, band gap energy was determined to be 3.85 eV for MOF-808 and 2.83 eV for MS-0.5 composite.

Mott-Schottky (M−S) technique was applied to determine the conduction band (CB) position of MOF-808. According to Fig. 7 a, the M−S plot has a positive slope. This observation verified that MOF-808 can be classified as an n-type semiconductor, with a flat band potential of −0.49 V (vs. SCE). Typically, the flat band of an n-type semiconductor is 0.1 V more positive than the CB potential [37]. Therefore, the CB potential of MOF-808 assumed to be −0.34 V (vs. NHE). Based on the Eg = EVB-ECB equation, the potential of the EVB was determined to be 3.51 V (vs. NHE).

The EIS Nyquist plots (Fig. 7b) clearly showed that the MS-0.5 composite had a smaller arc radius in comparison with the MOF-808. The smaller radian means the lower charge transfer resistance [38]. Therefore, the MS-0.5 composite exhibited more effective photoinduced charge separation and smaller charge-transfer resistance.

Before assessing the photocatalytic performance of the prepared materials, a higher photocatalytic efficiency is expected for the composite due to the following reasons. First, the UV–Vis DRS spectra (Fig. 6a) showed that MOF-808 could only absorb UV light, while the composite had an absorption peak in the visible light area, extending its optical response. Second, the composite had a narrower bandgap, which led to the participation of more photogenerated charges and enhanced photocatalytic efficiency [39]. Third, the EIS Nyquist plots (Fig. 7b) revealed that the composite had a smaller arc radius, resulting in the efficient separation of electron-hole pairs and preventing the recombination.

The RhB photodegradation tests were analyzed using hydrogen peroxide and visible light over prepared materials. Fig. 8 a illustrated that MS-0.5 had the best photocatalytic activity with 77 % removal efficiency of RhB (16 % adsorption and 61 % degradation), followed by MS-1 (10 % adsorption and 57 % degradation), MS-0.2 (18 % adsorption and 35 % degradation) and MS-1.5 (9 % adsorption and 43 % degradation). Also, pristine MOF-808 showed negligible removal efficiency (only 30 %), while all the composites exhibited higher photocatalytic activity in the same condition. These findings confirmed that S,N-CQDs were able to improve photocatalytic efficiency of MOF-808; however, the excess of S,N-CQDs on the surface of MOF-808 led to lower photocatalytic activity. Due to the fact that S,N-CQDs were able to absorb visible light, the higher content of these quantum dots on the surface of MOF-808 resulted in a competition for visible light absorption. Thus, the availability of visible light photons to produce electron-hole pairs decreased, resulting in a reduction of RhB degradation efficiency [40]. In addition, based on the results, composites with higher content of S,N-CQDs exhibited lower adsorption of RhB, which MOF-808 showing the highest and MS-1.5 showing the lowest adsorption. Because the existence of S,N-CQDs can reduce the specific surface area of MOF-808, which is in consistent with the BET results. Although the BET surface area of MOF-808 was 2.66 times greater than that of MS-0.5 composite, the difference in RhB adsorption was not significant. As depicted in Fig. S2, MOF-808 exhibited a positively charged (+60.7 mV) zeta potential, whereas the composite displayed a negatively charged (-30.9 mV) zeta potential. These results indicated that the surface decoration of MOF-808 with S,N-CQDs significantly reduced the zeta potential, even rendering it negative, likely due to the presence of oxygenic groups on the surface of S,N-CQDs [33]. RhB is a cationic dye that can be adsorbed better on the surface of photocatalysts having strong negative charge on their surface because of the electrostatic interactions. Consequently, a greater negative charge on the surface of the photocatalyst resulted in higher RhB adsorption and ultimately improved photocatalytic efficiency [41]. Therefore, on the one hand, the BET surface area of MOF-808 is 2.66 times greater than that of MS-0.5 composite, but on the other hand, the composite has a strong negative charge on its surface. These two factors influenced RhB adsorption and competed with each other, resulting in no significant difference in adsorption capacity.

The other degradation systems were evaluated to determine the effect of certain factors on RhB decomposition, as shown in Fig. 8b. Results showed that when visible light irradiation was combined with H2O2 in the absence of a photocatalyst (H2O2 + Vis), just 11 % of RhB was decomposed, probably due to the poor conversion of H2O2 to •OH. In addition, relatively poor RhB removal (44 %) was observed in the system consisting of MS-0.5 composite and visible light without oxidant (MS-0.5 + vis). Also, the addition of S,N-CQDs to the system and exposure to the oxidant and visible light (S,N-CQDs + H2O2 + Vis) was only able to remove 15 % of the RhB. This result showed that unsupported S,N-CQDs could not proceed the photodegradation of RhB, mainly owing to the fast recombination of charge carriers. In addition, the MS-0.5 + H2O2 system showed only around 20 % removal of RhB in the dark condition. Thus, the MS-0.5 + H2O2 + Vis system showed the highest RhB degradation efficiency because of the synergy among visible light, MS-0.5 composite, and H2O2. Fig. 8c and d demonstrated the efficiency of RhB removal by steadily decreasing the intensity of RhB absorption peak at 554 nm over time for MOF-808 + H2O2 + vis and MS-0.5 + H2O2 + vis systems, respectively.

To provide a more comprehensive evaluation of the efficiency of various photocatalytic systems, the Langmuir-Hinshelwood pseudo-first-order kinetic model was employed, which is widely recognized for the degradation of different types of organic molecules. The pseudo-first-order equation expressed as ln(C/C0) = -k1t, and the numerical values of k1 for different photocatalytic systems were found directly from the linear curve of - ln(C/C0) versus t plot (Fig. 9 a) [42]. According to the results, MS-0.5 + H2O2 + Vis demonstrated the highest rate constant equals to 0.0063 min−1, which is 6.93, 3.56, and 29.67 times higher than MOF-808 + H2O2 + Vis, MS-0.5 + Vis, and S,N-CQDs + H2O2 + Vis systems, respectively.

By adding different amounts of H2O2 to the solution while keeping all other factors constant, the impact of H2O2 concentration on the degradation of RhB was examined. Fig. 10 a demonstrates that by increasing the H2O2 concentration from 5 to 20 mM, the degradation efficiency increased because when H2O2 concentration rises, more OH radicals were produced. However, when H2O2 concentration was increased from 20 mM to 30 mM, the removal effectiveness decreased from 77 % to 67 %, most likely due to the interaction of excess H2O2 with OH radicals and resulted in the production of H2O and O2 molecules [43].

The photocatalytic tests for various concentrations of RhB were performed in the presence of MS-0.5 and H2O2 (20 mM) to evaluate the impact of dye concentration (Fig. 10b). Degradation efficiency of RhB at concentrations of 20, 30, and 40 ppm over the MS-0.5 composite was 77 %, 62 %, and 50 %, respectively, demonstrating a decrease in degradation with increasing RhB concentration. The effectiveness of catalyst degradation was influenced by the production of OH radicals on the surface of catalyst and the potential of these radicals interacting with dye molecules. The generation of hydroxyl radicals (·OH) requires many photons to enter the solution. Increase in the initial RhB concentration can reduce photon absorption by the catalyst [44]. Therefore, fewer OH radicals were produced, which resulted in reducing RhB degradation efficiency.

The effect of photocatalyst dosage on RhB degradation is depicted in Fig. 10c. When the amount of photocatalyst increased up to 30 mg, the degradation efficiency of RhB was enhanced to 89 % because there would be more active sites in the photocatalytic system. On the contrary, at higher dosage of photocatalyst, the solution became cloudy and the excess amount of photocatalyst caused light scattering, which limited the light penetration onto the surface of MS-0.5 composite [45].

The pH of solution is an essential factor in determining the performance of a photocatalyst, thus, the photocatalytic activity of MS-0.5 composite was investigated in a broad pH range (pH = 2–10). It was observed that the removal efficiency decreased under strongly acidic-basic conditions, as illustrated in Fig. 10d. Degradation efficiency decreased to 67 % at pH = 2 because the extra amount of H+ can influence the charge of S,N-CQDs due to the protonation of carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine groups [46]. Therefore, the electrostatic repulsion force limited the adsorption of the RhB molecules as a cationic dye. Also, the degradation efficiency decreased to 64 % at pH = 10, most likely due to the relatively low oxidation potential of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and the partially decomposition of H2O2 into O2 and H2O [47]. Therefore, the highest RhB photodegradation can be achieved at neutral pH.

The photocatalytic performance of MS-0.5 composite was compared under two different light sources. As shown in Fig. 11 , the photodegradation activity of MS-0.5 composite under sunlight irradiation is higher than visible light and 95 % of RhB was degraded during the short time of 150 min. The probable reason for this observation is that MOF-808 can be also excited by small portion of UV rays from sun and acted as a photosensitizer. The mechanism and differences were explained in details in section 3.3.

To further study the photodegradation mechanism of RhB in MS-0.5 + H2O2 + Vis system, the trapping tests of reactive species were carried out. Some scavengers like isopropanol (IPA), Na2EDTA, CCl4, and benzoquinone (BQ) can be used to quench •OH, h+, e-, •O2 reactive species, respectively [48]. Fig. 12 a demonstrated that CCl4 and BQ did not impact the degradation of RhB. However, both IPA and Na2EDTA significantly decreased the degradation efficiency, which indicated that •OH and h+ played the essential roles in this photocatalytic system. The proposed mechanism for the photocatalytic behavior of the MS-0.5 composite was presented based on the findings from the EIS, M−S analyses, and Tauc plots. The hypothesized mechanism based on the scavenging and photocatalytic test results was presented in Scheme 2 . The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of MOF-808, as depicted in Fig. 6a, indicated a lack of significant absorption peaks in the visible light region. Consequently, under visible light irradiation, S,N-CQDs with a narrow band gap of 1.95 eV (Fig. S1) can absorb photons of energy (hν) equal to or greater than the band gap energy (Eq. (1)) [23]. Since, electroconductive S,N-CQDs are good electron acceptors and donors [49], the photoexcited electrons can move from the CB of S,N-CQDs to the CB of MOF-808 (Eq. (2)). Therefore, under visible light illumination, S,N-CQDs operated as a photosensitizer and donated electrons to the CB of MOF-808; thus, S,N-CQDs effectively broaden the photo-response range of MS-x composites to visible light. This electron migration led to reduced charge recombination and improved photocatalytic activity, which was consistent with the findings in section 3.2. The redox potential of •OH/H2O2 (0.38 V vs. NHE) is more positive than the CB potential of MOF-808 (-0.34 V), therefore, it is probable that H2O2 trapped the electrons on the CB of MOF-808 and produce •OH (a necessary radical to decompose the dye molecules into CO2 and H2O) (Eqs. (3) and (4)) [37]. Based on the results of scavenger trapping experiment, remaining h+ on the VB of S,N-CQDs can directly react with RhB to produce degraded products (Eq. (5)).

Under direct sunlight irradiation, in addition to the above-mentioned mechanism, MOF-808 can also absorb photons with shorter wavelengths (λ < 400 nm) and be excited (Eq. (6)). It was observed that S,N-CQDs were able to serve as electron acceptors, which can effectively trap photogenerated electrons from the conduction band of MOF-808 (Eq. (7)). The electrons of S,N-CQDs were anticipated to react with H2O2 to produce •OH and OH– (Eq. (8)). The remaining positive holes (h+) on the VB of MOF-808 had three roles: (1) it can react with RhB molecules directly (Eq. (5), (2) it can produce hydroxyl radicals through interaction with OH– (•OH/OH–=1.99 V) (Eq. (9)) [50], and (3) because the VB potential of MOF-808 (3.51 V) is comparatively higher than the redox potential of •OH/H2O (2.27 V), H2O molecules can interact with h+ to produce •OH; thus, water molecules were able to produce hydroxyl radicals directly, as represented by Eq. (10) [51].
(1) S,N-CQDs + hν (λ > 400 nm) → e- + h+
(2) MOF-808 + e- → MOF-808(e-)
(3) MOF-808(e-) + H2O2 → •OH + OH–
(4)
•OH + RhB → Degraded products
(5) h+ + RhB → Degraded products
(6) MOF-808 + hν (λ < 400 nm) → e- + h+
(7) S,N-CQDs + e- → S,N-CQDs(e-)
(8) S,N-CQDs(e-) + H2O2 → •OH + OH–
(9) OH– + h+ → •OH
(10) h+ + H2O → •OH + H+

The reusability of the MS-0.5 composite was tested by using the composite in four photocatalytic cycles of RhB degradation. To ensure that all remaining adsorbed RhB was removed from the composite surface, the recovered MS-0.5 composite was soxhlet with methanol for 24 h after each cycle and then thermally activated for the upcoming cycles. As shown in Fig. 12b, 76 % of RhB was degraded at the 4th cycle, indicating that the MS-0.5 composite was reusable and efficient for successive dye photodegradation experiments. Also, the stability of MS-0.5 composite was confirmed after forth cycle using XRD and FT-IR analyses. The photocatalyst's crystallinity did not change throughout the photodegradation tests, as shown by the XRD pattern of the recovered MS-0.5 composite (Fig. 12c), indicating a good match with the fresh one. Furthermore, The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 12d) of the fourth recovered MS-0.5 composite was identical to the fresh one.

Additionally, the photodegradation of RhB under visible light irradiation was compared with other MOF-based photocatalysts in photodegradation of organic dyes. Table 1 showed that the MS-0.5 composite was similar and even more efficient than giving examples in dye photodegradation. In addition, the light source in this study (LED lamps) was considerably more economical than other works (Xe, halogen, and tungsten lamps), demonstrating that the MS-0.5 composite can be a promising choice for photocatalytic water remediation system.

In conclusion, a simple solvent-deposition technique was utilized to fabricate novel and effective composites of MOF-808 with S,N-CQDs. The physicochemical characteristics and electrical/optical performance of prepared materials were examined by FT-IR, XRD, FE-SEM, HR-TEM, N2 adsorption–desorption, EDX, UV–Vis DRS, EIS and Mott-Schottky analyses. Studies of optical characteristics of MOF-808 and MS-0.5 composite revealed that the improved photocatalytic activity of MS-0.5 composite could be attributed to the photoexcitation of S,N-CQDs under visible light illumination and photogenerated electrons could transfer to the surface of MOF-808. In comparison, MOF-808 can absorb and be excited by photons with a shorter wavelength of < 400 nm when exposed to sunlight. In general, the results showed that S,N-CQDs decorated on the surface of MOF-808 could increase the visible light absorption of MOF-808 which led to enhancement of photocatalytic degradation of RhB. To determine the optimal conditions, the effects of photocatalyst and H2O2 dosage, pH of the solution, and RhB concentration were examined. MS-0.5 composite showed the best photocatalytic activity, and the composite was able to remove 89 % of RhB exposed to visible light and 95 % under sunlight illumination with 20 mM of H2O2, 30 mg of photocatalyst and 20 mg/L of RhB at pH = 6. Radical quenching experiments showed that photogenerated holes and OH radicals were crucial in the photodegradation process. In addition, the hypothesized mechanism suggested that free radicals have different responsibilities in photocatalytic reactions. After four recycling cycles, the MS-0.5 composite only had a 13 % loss of photoactivity, which demonstrated the high stability of this composite.
